speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Gerald Ingle took place at the Latah County Courthouse on October 7, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I was doing the impression. It made it pretty rough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Three at once?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: My dad was president of the Bank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a big bear. Well, maybe we should start back with growing up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You grew up on a farm, right?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A big bear.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your parents had been here considerably early, hadn't they?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, the place that I grew up on was my grandfather's old home. He was a big bear. Then when my father took over the farm, my grandfather built another house over on another piece of land. We still have that place. In fact, I own that place now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember the farming being like when you were just a kid? Was it still really pioneering, pretty much? Or had it passed through?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, we broke out a lot of land there too after I was a kid and helping.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Of course, in those days, we used horses. We never got tractor, I think in 1923 or fours when we bought the first tractor and farm the horses up to the end.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The kids expected to do a lot of that work, a lot of the breaking.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: We helped pile brush and cut brush and stuff like that. We did some of that after I was big enough to get around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you were going to try to characterize what the condition of the average farmer was when you were still pretty young, you were born in 10, so figure it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Between the teens and the 20s, how were they doing?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, they were pretty hard up, really.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: The country had a lot more people in. A lot of people had just on 168 years of land. Some had a little more and some had smaller places. They milked cows and sold cream and sold eggs and sold butter those days. They didn't have cream here so much. They churned the cream into butter and sold butter.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: That was how they paid their store bills for the food bills they got out of the store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that their main source of money or did they get much?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: They got a fair bit off the crops. The main source was their crop, but as far as the household needs they needed in the kitchen to put food on the table. A lot of that came from the sale of things they bought in the grocery store, the sale of butter and eggs and so forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They traded in Kendrick, right?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Right, in that area, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they go mostly to the long store or is there another store?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, there was a long store and there was a Stanton store and there was a Dameril store. There were the three stores when I was a kid. The long store is there where Erickson's is. The Stanton store was there where the drug store is. The Dameril store is where Abraham's hardware is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often would your family make it in there, Kendrick?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, I think probably once a week or so probably.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far? How far?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: It was at nine miles from town. My dad bought a car, first car in 1914, so I was only four years old when he bought the car. You know, it used to be back years ago that I remember a kid, most of the farmers went to town on Saturday afternoon, took the day off and did their trading and so forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be just the manna family or would their wife come?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, some of them would take their wives and so forth. Later on they did more and did early days, but probably earlier they were just the man. Maybe take the kids or one of the kids along with them to town. I remember I used to go to town with my dad, buy myself a him, maybe her brother the next time or something, we had to take her turn to go to town. That's why I treated him to go to town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you do when you were there as a kid in town?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I just go off the field, I remember now.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I remember one time I went down, the first time I ever drank soda pop, got me a bottle of lemon pop. I thought that was really something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he do much visiting with other guys when you went to town?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh yes, he was on the board of directors of the cooperative warehouse. It was the Kenner Grossnip Company. That's one of the oldest cooperatives in the county. The Kendrick Roachdale.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Roachdale?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: He was on that board of directors for 30 or 40 years I guess. I don't know, born out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a farmer owned cooperative or was it private?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, farmer owned cooperative. It's a little bit different, the co -ops they have nowadays, each farmer had to put up for a $100 stock. Now the laws are a little different I guess. Of course they could do business with anybody, but these stockholders got to receive dividends from their business returns.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder how broad the ownership was, how many people had put in the $100 box?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I don't know.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: There must have been. I've seen that old stockholders list. There must have been 75 to 100 people. Real broad participation. Yeah, because at that time I'd say there was a lot more people in the rural area, in smaller farms. It was at a pretty good size and he was on that board of directors. So he always had to go down once a month at the meeting for the board of directors of the Hiddicore -Stale Company.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that come out of a farmer's union kind of activity?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, it was sort of a... I imagine, I remember it used to have a farmer's union too, probably originally it first started probably from that. I remember Dad used to have a big farmer's union sign on the garage there when I was a kid. I don't know whether there was connection to that or not, but I just imagine that was sort of an off shot of a cooperative deal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what your father's attitudes were about bettering the position of the farmer in a political sense?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, I always felt Dad was pretty interested in developing the country and a real good attitude about developing. He used to get pretty irked about some of these people that held back on things. He liked to see good schools and he liked to see good roads. I remember several times when he had gone to Boise with different people trying to get them to fix a certain road, to get the states to fix a certain road. He was pretty much on... I liked to see developments made. That's probably why it rubbed off on me over here being in public service.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have much idea of how he became disposed towards public service?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because it seems like a lot of fellas who were farmers didn't get out beyond their own place in terms of their civic interests. You gave him that inclination. I don't know.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I just...working in this cooperative grain elevator, and he was also a director in the bank for several years there too. And he worked with people all the time and so on. But I know he was on the school board too when I was in school. And we used to have a local telephone company. He was always an officer in our local cooperative telephone company. That was established about... I guess that was established about 19, 8 or 9. And he was chairman of that a lot and secretary of that a lot. I think he just sort of grew up with it. He sort of liked to work with people too. Then he got to be county commissioner one time too. Oh, about... I was in 42 and 3. He was only one term. I wonder where King, a nickname King... King came from his...that was his name, King David Ingle. He had an uncle. His dad's brother was named King Ingle too. I see. So that was his first name. Yeah. King David Ingle. His uncle's name was the same. And I think there was a father up the ladder that was somebody named King.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How hard do you feel that he worked on the place?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: He worked hard. Dad really worked hard. And we all...we had... We were a little bigger farmers than most of them when I was a kid. We had probably 400 acres, 350, 400 acres. And we always had a hired man. When I was a kid, I always had a hired girl to help in the house when those kids were going up. Year -round. Yeah. Well, actually in the summer. Sometimes in the winter we wouldn't have. And we had cattle and some cattle, baked cattle, milk cows too. And sometimes we'd have hired men in the winter. But most of us would generally start early in the spring and go to say November or December.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that 3, 400 acres, how much of that were you farming? Would that be almost close to that?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh, let's see. I can figure it was actually about, I suppose there was over 300... There was over 300 acres of farmland, 350 problems. And of that, did you own all that land or did you rent... Well, he ran part of it and some of it he owned. And part of it was my grandfather's land. See, my grandfather never died until 1934 and he lived, I think it was, 34 or 5, I believe it was. And he lived only half a mile from us. And grandfather had 320 acres of this land. See, that we farmed, that wasn't all cultivated, but there was two quarters. Then my dad owned an 80 acres, joining that. And my aunt, which, these two pieces were part of the old estate, my aunt, my dad's sister had an 80 acres. And we farmed those two 80s. That was originally part of my grandfather's acreage.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was pretty much all family that... Yeah, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Family owned, what was he owned, even if he didn't own it himself?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, did he need bigger equipment than most of the farmers at the time to farm that? Or was it just a lot longer work?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, I don't know why they had bigger equipment. Just more people and more pieces of equipment. Two bottom gangplow, you know, was a big plow in those days in that area. And maybe they'd run two of them, see, and take six horses to the land. And lots of times he used to plow with a gangplow. One would show a foot burner walking plow with three horses on the end. I started around knowing one of those when I was 10 years old a little bit. I was pretty good size and age, up to 11, 12. I ran it 12. We'd have the gangplow ahead and the foot burner behind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work a whole day on a foot burner?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh, yes. After I got 12, 13 years old. I remember the first time I ever run one when I was 10 years old, we plowed the garden tonight. I wanted to run that. I could just reach up like hell and hell. I wanted to drive that team horse and run that plow. And so dad let me drive a few rounds. And then he just went off and let me finish it. I got a little tired before he got done. But after I was up 12, 15 years old, I ran it all the time. Then I got so ready to gangplow. Then in those days we bound. Dad didn't like to run a binder very well. So as soon as I was big enough, I imagine when I was 13, 14, I started running a binder for harvest. And my brother shopped. He was a little younger. I remember one year there, the poor eyes started running the binder. The kids were pretty small. My dad's brother was Dwight Inkel. He lived with my grandfather. Dad had us three boys shopping. A lot of them fell down, but we used to have to shop the grain. And then a year or two after that, I started running the binder. And then my brother did the shopping.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I had heard that in the early days, that all the work that the kids had to do on the place has really gotten away of their going to school. They would lay out quite a bit in the early days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By the time you were growing up, did it conflict with you going to school at home?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: No, not in our home. It did with some families. But my dad taught education in Portland. We had to melt cows before we went to school and do chores. If we had to feed the hogs, certainly there was four of us in our family. Each one of us had a job to do, and that had to be done before we went to school. We were late. But he was real.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he didn't believe in having to play out with it.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Only time, once in a while, like second of fall, we'd have wet fall and it was thrashing grain. We used to have the company thrashing, machines coming thrash. Once in a while then, if it was late fall and short of labor, trying to get things done in the fast, maybe we'd stay out a day or two when we had the thrash. But as far as my dad is concerned, he believes in us being in school every day. In fact, most of the time when I went to school, I had a perfect attendance record.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really? Where did you go? Was there just school on the ridge there?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, I had a little local school called the Taney School. It was built in 1892. And that was one of the first schools built on the ridge. There were five schools at that time on the ridge, but ours was the central school. It was in the center of the ridge, and it was actually the biggest school of the five.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many kids were you going there?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I think the maximum amount at any time was 35. I remember the year I started the school, first grade, there was 28 that year, and then the year or so after was 35. But I think it ran up between that number, 25 to 35. I might have had a few more once in a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go there clear through the eighth?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think of it? Of how good of a word you got in it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I think it was amazing.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: These school districts were poor, and most of the teachers they had were people that had gone to their first year or a half a year or something. A lot of them came from the Lewis and Normal, just going. Some of them once in a while would have just went to summer school and took an examination to get their certificate, and some of them one year, and some of them two years. A big lot of them were their first school, you know, because they didn't get too good at pay. But I think it was amazing the kids had learned as much as they did. And I think one reason why they did, you see, they always had the whole eight grades, and they had this recitation bench up in front, you know. And you listened to the other classes all the way through from the third to the eighth grade, and you couldn't help but listen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it really gets drilled into you by being a expert?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I think probably that was one reason why some of the one -room schools were so successful, I think probably because of hearing this over and over and over and over and over again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I think that's something that I think we had to be aware of, When you were a little kid going to school there, do you remember there being any real big kids that hadn't had that much learning that were back to them?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I remember three boys that was in the eighth grade, and they were six foot tall and they were big guys, and they used to really give us little kids the devil's. I don't think they ever did finish the eighth grade, but they had to go to their parents' would send us in and wanted to get through the eighth grade. So these big guys like that, when I was a year I went to school, they would work in the fall and come and go to school in winter, and then they'd get in the spring. So they actually never did, but the folks kept wanting them to get past that eighth grade examination. And boy, they were mean to us little kids sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it sounds like they weren't growing up in themselves even if they were big.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: They were just kids. They were kids. I remember one time when I hung me up in a sack behind a horse out in the barn, you'd see a lot of the kids, a few of the kids, rode a horse back because they had to come by way to school, and they had a barn there on the school out where they'd keep their horses. Remember I also never will forget that, I guess. This kid hung me, I scared me to beat the band. He hung you in the sack and he left you there. The Gunny sack hung me there for a little while back behind the horse. They had a lot of fun, but I was so scared, but they didn't leave me there at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old was the kids? Maybe about 18 or something?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I imagine, since 1718.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of embarrassing them two to be?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: They shouldn't have been in school anymore. No, it was sort of bad for them because actually I think they were bigger than a teacher. She had quite a time with those boys sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have trouble with the discipline and order?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: With those older ones. Some of the teachers did, but some of those gals had taught them. I tell you, she could straighten them out. I mean, these bad kids. I think those big kids were... I remember those three boys I first spoke of when I was in first grade were the three biggest in our school course. Eventually I got to be one of those big boys. I got to be in the grade, I guess. But they were pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your mother not teach during that whole time?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: No, she quit teaching when she got married, when she married dad. She had taught this school. She taught three other schools on this ridge prior to marrying my dad. She was a schoolteacher, so she saw that we studied our lessons. We had to study every night at home after we got our chores done and our supperies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she met her by while she was teaching?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Did she know when she was... No, she met him while she was teaching. She lived over on Little Bear Ridge just across the gang from Big Bear. I don't think he really knew her until she came over there teaching. He might have known who she was. Her name was Hup and he lived on the point of Little Bear Ridge. They might have, but he never really knew her until she... Never went with her or anything until she got to teaching over there. I did ask you how... if your father worked hard. So I should ask you how hard you think she worked. She worked hard. She always can through and take... We always had a big garden. That was one of us boys' jobs, hoeing the garden. I remember one... This was a sort of... It's about my brother. You know, one time we used to have to hoe in the garden and he'd do something. She'd kick him out of the garden. One day dad would rain a little bit and dad was out with us boys hoeing the garden. First thing my brother did was chop off a stalk of corn. And he'd blow him out and say, if he did that again, he would sure... give him a good tan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, why aren't you going to kick me out of the garden?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: He says, that's what mom does when I do something like that. But she worked hard. She made butter and... from the milk cream that we got from the cows. Did lots of canning. And she was a pretty good seamstress. She just so... made clothes for us too. My sister, she always made all her dresses and so forth. She was a pretty good seamstress.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she ever go back to teaching?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: No. She... I was calling a few times, you know, substance a little bit. She never went back to teaching. She always sort of wanted to, but she always had plenty of work to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't that pretty much the pattern of young women in those days?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah. They'd teach and they'd marry and then that was it. That was it, yeah. No, very few of them went back to school. Some of them went back to teaching. Some of them would, after their family was grown or something, maybe, go on. But generally, they stayed home to care. They ran the house home to care of the family. It seemed to be the pattern of those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For you to get to school in Kendrick, was that a hardship after you got out of the 8th grade?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: After got out of the 8th grade, first year or two, I rode horseback to Kendrick in the morning. I mean, in the fall and then in the spring when the weather was good and then in the wintertime, I stayed in town. Then the second year, I was big enough that I was 14, I think it was, or so there. I started driving a car, drove a car in the fall and in the spring. I drove down that old grade and up between it was put it in low gear and stay up in low gear always. He got the top of the 3 -mile grade coming up.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I guess probably, I did a little bit the first year when I was a freshman in high school, but when the weather got rainy and slick, then I rode horseback. After that first year, we never did ride horseback again. We always drove in the fall and then we stayed in town the wintertime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a second car that you had?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: No, that was a family car. That was a little 1920 Dodge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that grade ever dangerous, real dangerous to be on sometimes?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've driven it now, I wonder, even on the winter or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You haven't driven that old grade?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: No, I drove the one off Little Bear Ridge into Kendrick. This one was on the other side and it was dangerous. You take somebody down, I guess you can get up and down. Now there's some people that live part way up on it, but it was really a dangerous grade. You take a dangerous grade down, you had to be used to driving on it. There was just once in a while a turn out where you could see somebody coming, you'd have to wait for that wait to pass or whoever. People drove it all the time were pretty good about that. They see somebody coming, they were close to the turn out they'd wait or vice versa. Sometimes you'd have to back up to them, but it was a pretty dangerous grade really. You'd slip right straight down the canyon a lot of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the winter, say, how did you handle when there was snow on top and nothing down in Kendrick?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: You couldn't really take a sled down there in the early days all the way if there was no snow in Kendrick. No, it was pretty rough. It seemed like we used to have more snow then. There was another grade on the other side that would, I'd call it a pine creek grade. They used to go down sometimes in the winter and they'd leave their sled at the bottom of the grade and go into town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Walk in?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, or something. Take one of the horses and ride in or something. But lots of times they used a buggy, you know, and you'd go and get the road broken and use the buggy on top of the snow to cut the tracks if they couldn't get to town with a sled. When I was in high school, lots of times we didn't think anything about when, Friday night when school was out to walk home. I'd kill kids now to walk that eight or nine miles. About eight miles or eight and a half miles and get home. And then we'd, once upon a time, we'd walk down Sunday night.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: We didn't think anything about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you never stayed in town except for the winter?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Winter time was on top. But then you'd walk the nine miles and it took quite a while for just some time. Well, we used to walk to the top of the grade and we'd take the shortcut and we'd run down the grade. We could make it an hour and twenty minutes probably. Sometimes the folks would take us to the top of the grade. And there was two shortcuts on the grade where we could not follow the road and go straight down the hill and we'd take the shortcuts to town. But coming back up when we'd walk home, the shortcuts were steeper. We couldn't go too fast.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the transition for you from the country school to the Kendrick School? Was it a big change?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yes, it was. You know, first for a little bit, it didn't take me too long to get oriented. But it was quite a change at first because, you know, you're used to the small group and so forth. As I say, I sure felt like a big pebble in a lake for a little while, but it didn't take me long to get oriented.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you know, I've heard people talk about how when they get into the town and then mix in with the town kids, they really feel backwards at first because, you know, the town kids are a little more slick.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: And the big difference would seem to be with Moscow and the people on it down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did you feel that first?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I felt that first, but I don't know. I was pretty easy to make friends. And I got along real good with the town kids. They sort of used to call us country jerks and that kind of stuff, you know. But it didn't take long, of course. With Kendrick, there were so many of them that came in from the rural areas too anyway. So there's a lot of country kids. So actually, I imagine the percent was probably 50 -50, so it didn't take very long to get knowing each other and working together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were in high school, was it mostly just studies or was there a lot of what today seems to be so big in high school sports and the extracurricular activities?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, we had, basically the main thing was there studies. We had basketball and baseball, and they never had football. They never had football. And Kendrick, until the year after I went out, got out of high school. I graduated in spring of 28. They never started football that fall, but we had baseball and basketball. And I played basketball. I wasn't much of a baseball, but I was on the basketball team for three years, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of socializing between the boys and the girls when you were growing up? Was there much opportunity for it?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, quite a little, I guess, we call it. And we used to have, in our local community up there, every winter, we used to have what they call a literary society. And every Saturday night or every other Saturday night, certain groups would be in charge of putting on a program for that evening. And then they'd have their eats and stuff afterwards. And then they used to have these old basket suppers to raise them, auction off boxes and pie suppers they'd offer, off pies and stuff like that, to raise money for different things in the community. And we always had a big deal at Christmas time. We used to have what they call a UB Church, which was just a little waste. United Brethren Church is just a little waste from our schoolhouse. All the schools would go together and put on the Christmas program, and they would go around the community and collect money to buy a treat. So everybody got a sack of candy and something. And every school would be in charge of putting on a certain part of their program. Some would have some dialogues, some would have some speeches and so forth. And it was really quite an festive occasion that Christmas, the old Christmas community, Christmas tree.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you had these literaries and that kind of thing, did you have dances after them?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh, dancing was a taboo. We could play skip and loo and that kind of stuff. But dancing, that was sort of bad for some of them. So they could play games.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a divided opinion at the time?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh yeah. Yeah, that's right. And then some of them, they generally just compromised.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you played these dancing, singing games?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah. Party games. Party games. I remember, I used to think it was sort of funny that they could play skip and loo, but they couldn't dance. But later on in the early 20s, they built a community hall there, and they used to have public dances over there. And then after they got that hall, they used to have their Christmas tree in the community hall. It's still standing there. It's on the back. It's on one of the places I own.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the, what did they call me?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: A gentleman. Yeah, I see them all. Yeah, that's all my place. Yeah, that hall got a pretty good -sized draw, too. I've heard people from Bovill say anything over there. Oh yeah, those public dances, they used to come from all over. They used to have some real wingdings there. Yeah. Yeah, that's what I heard. But my folks wouldn't let me go to the dances. We could go to other things over there, but my dad, there's one thing, he was sorry against dancing. I never got to dancing until I really, much until I came to college. Then I took dancing lessons and started dancing. He was real strict about dancing. And he was sort of strict about cards. We couldn't play, when we were younger, we couldn't play even Peanutthor or something. But anyway, after we got up in high school, we played anyway, and he accepted it. But you know, he's like dancing and cards and drinking and all that kind of stuff are in together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that was, for him, was that a religious belief, or was it a moral, just morals?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, religious and well moral, I suppose. His mother was real strict, I guess. And she died when he was a teenager. And I guess she left quite an impression on him. And so he was just that way. My mother's a lot more broad -minded about it, but he was pretty strict.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he never had a drink?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: He never drank at all. No, he was a teetotaler.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he must have been for prohibition when it came in.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: He wasn't one of those real rapping ones to go out and preach it. I mean, if you was with him and wanted to drink, he let you drink. I mean, he just didn't do it himself. I had heard that the dance hall on the ridge when it came in caused some conflict with the people who were really against dancing anymore. Yes, it did. And still, on the other hand, they actually got so they had Sunday school in that community hall part of the time. They'd have Sunday school next morning after they had a dance there on Saturday night. So each group learned to live with it. And pretty funny, in the local community, as I used to look back, they'd fight like cats and dogs and women themselves. When somebody had bad luck or a burnout or something, they'd have trouble with all the neighbors coming in and helping to forget all their petty peeves then. They worked for each other. When they built this community hall, it was a donation labor. And everybody helped. I know my dad helped work on it too.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: But I guess he sort of had the attitude, the people wanted to do that type of thing. It was all right, but he just didn't do it himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he didn't like his family to be doing some of those things, like drinking and dancing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would you say that it was, though, pretty much divided into two groups, those that accepted drinking and dancing and those that were against it? Is that pretty much the way it worked?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I guess it was, but still...

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I mean, it's common in a lot of these small communities. But, you know, I imagine so. I guess it was. Some of these people that drank and tore around would come to church in Sunday school too. Still wasn't as straight. No, it wasn't as straight down the... Well, I imagine there were some people who were really good at dancing and not drinking. Yeah, right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the UB Church? Did most of the people from the area go to that church?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, they had two churches. The one on the other end of the ridge was Luther. Most of the people at one time lived on the lower end of the ridge were Luther. And they had the Bethany Luther Church. And then I think most of the rest of the people, even though they belonged to other denominations, would come to church there and could take part in Sunday school and so forth, because it was the church. Later on, that church was... The United Brethren wanted to sell it. It sort of died out, and they couldn't furnish the minister there. It didn't come much. So my grandfather and my dad and my uncle bought the building. And my grandfather donated the land, and so the community tore it down and built it, built it in the church they have there by the cemetery from the old lumber, of course with the new... But basically the building was built with the timbers and stuff from that old church. And now it's a community church. Yeah, it's not used very much. Once in a while I have funerals. Once in a while I have a service there. But it's not near the people. It used to be... It used to have quite a group come. It used to have regular revival meetings and that type of thing there. Well, I guess they did it after they first built the church, but the old church used to have a lot of that. They had some of that after the new church was built. I was able to move there in the 30s. Let me do that. Look, the rivals last very long. Oh, they'd have two weeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they well attended?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Pretty well. I remember when we used to go on these kids, we used to walk down to that church, so it was a little over a mile from our place to the old church. We used to walk to church that night in the wintertime. Well, how much...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about what the revival itself was like? Was the preaching pretty strong?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, as I remember it was, it seemed like... Yeah, I think it was pretty sincere and strong. It affected some people again. Some... Because I had heard that the way it affected a lot of people was very emotional. There was a lot of... Yeah, there was some of them. Well, you still have that. You have your emotional groups too. Some churches are a lot more emotional than others, but before you had the whole bunch, there was some of them that affected a lot more. Did others.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Then we used to have their church parties too. That was part of the community deal sometimes too. Which was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of party would that be?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh, that'd just be a church dinner or something. We'd call them bazaar now, I guess. Stuff like that. Get together, and maybe on a Sunday have a church dinner or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the people expected to go to church on Sunday?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: It seemed to me like the biggest part of them at that time. Went to church sometime during the year, probably didn't turn around. We had pretty good advances as I remember when I did. So maybe not every Sunday, though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about working on Sunday?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Was that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there a lot of people that wouldn't do any work on Sunday?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh, yeah. There were a lot of people that wouldn't do anything on Sunday. Some of the people that didn't go to church wouldn't work on Sunday. There were a few that worked on Sunday, but... That was one thing that my dad always said that there was one day for rest, and we never did work in the field on Sunday around our home. Of course, we had the chores and stuff like that to do, but going out and doing actually work out in the field and so forth, we never did do that. He always said we had one day off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a visiting day for you?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, we used to go visit the neighbors and go... We used to have family picnics in the summertime. It was a day that nearly... You either had company or went someplace and was company for somebody else. It seemed like a lot more of that than there is now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, when you say family picnic, it makes me wonder, too, when you socialized then, was it mostly with the family relatives?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I'm a big part of it.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: We had my uncles live there, and my grandfather was there, and my dad's sister married a famous mower, and they had a family. They lived close there. We were visiting back and forth among ourselves, but still, on the other hand, too, some of the neighbors would go there for dinner on Sunday, you know, or go someplace else on Sunday. I imagine half of it, nearly half of it was among the family itself, were relatives. We had some relatives lived in Moscow and some lived in Colfax, and then after we got to use them on our meals, we used to visit with them on Sundays, and they'd visit us. But the big part of it was family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All these relatives, had they had more than one family come here originally that was related?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, see, my grandfather came here. When he came, he brought his hard man with him, and his hard man homesteaded, and same as he did. So that family grew up being pretty close, you know. And then, my aunt married one of the neighbor boys when she was pretty young. She was about 10 or 12 when she came here, and I guess she got married when she was 16, so that started in with another family. So it didn't take long, and so you had... My dad, of my grandmother's angles, had three brothers who lived in Colfax, and there was another brother who lived on... No, cousins. Cousins, they were. A brother lived at Colfax, and their cousins, three of them lived at Colfax, and one lived in Deary, and one lived on the Upper End of the Ridge Sea. That came about from my grandmother's angles side. We had those relatives to visit, and they had families too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your grandfather, where did he come from?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: He came from originally Tennessee, then he went to Illinois. He was Illinois when he actually came from Idaho. He came, I guess, in the fall of 1883. At that time, I think it was 1883, at that time they had the railroad built as far as Colfax. They ran a car on the railroad and piled all their belongings on one end, and the family lived in one end of it. Well, moved down on the railroad. The story I always heard him say is that he and his hard man was with him, and he came the same time, brought his family. He had a wife and some children, and grandfather had three or four children at that time. Some say he ran into a horse. They ran into horses and rode over there and found this land where we are, and the others said he ran into a buggy, so I don't know which he did. Anyway, he got from Colfax over there, and they settled over on the Big Bear Ridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a homesteader, nobody else had been on the place.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, first he had a right for a preemption, and he went on the preemption first, and then this place that he homesteaded, somebody had spotted on it, and then gave it up, so then he filed on it. It was open, so my grandfather filed a homestead on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he did that because he did it that way, because he wanted to still have the right to homestead?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think probably. I don't know what the deal was then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he get the preemption too?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, he improved up on both of them.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Then my uncle, the oldest one of the family, there was a piece of land just north of grandfather's preemption, and it hadn't been taken when he got of age, so he homesteaded that. So that's how it was. My cousin's house, that place, and my grandfather's old preemption. He homesteaded now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear from him why the movement, why he lived in Tennessee, Illinois?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yes. He had asthma off of that. I guess he just had an awful time. Somebody told him if he'd go west, he wouldn't have it. He just blown his family up and decided to try it. I remember him saying, he never had asthma all his life after he got out here. I remember him saying as soon as he came over the mountains, he started to breathe. I think that was one of the things that brought him out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have an idea about how old he was when he came west?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, I could figure it out. He was 84 years old when he died in 1934.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he would have been about 32.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said he came in 82?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: 83. He would have been 33. He was a reasonable young man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he talk to you at all about what those real early pioneer conditions were?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh, yeah. He used to, after he moved down to this other place to build a house, and he moved down there, and he had his garden. He used to have us boys help put his wood in. He'd come up to the place every day. He'd walk across the field up to our house. He visited with me a lot. He used to sit and talk with us. It was really interesting, some of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How hard did he make it out to be when it was, you know, just these first set?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Really rough. I remember him saying the first year when they came here in the fallen, I guess it was 83. And they didn't have much to eat. The neighbors had lots of rutabagas, and they killed a bear. He said they used rutabagas and bear meat. Of course, they got a deer, I guess, too. He said it was a pretty tough winter. I guess they completely ran out of groceries early in the winter, early in the spring, and they still had a lot of snow. And there was one trip that they fixed up on old Bob's sled. I took the front runners off of the Bob's sled and put a team on it, put a box on it. So you just had one set of runners. They came to Moscow to get some groceries. That was the nearest place they could come get groceries. It took them two days to come to Moscow.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: They had to restock. But anyway, that's the first one they first came. They used to ever fall after that. I mean, after they got us down, they'd say, bring the team and wagon and try to leave early in the morning and get out to Moscow and get their winter supplies. And they used to try to get out here in time, get their buying done that day. And that night, they'd hitch up and go out to what's called Cornwall, and there's just a family named Hobart that lived out there. They were real good friends. They'd always go out there and camp the rest of that night and then drive back home the next day. I've already talked about that several times how they used to visit old Sam Hobart.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They would camp outside of their house?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I guess we'd take as many of them in as I could, but that was the stopping place for the people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this kind of deal was just going to be in the fall, right?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, that was the fall deal. They always did that fall shopping. They always made that one trip to town. Maybe sometimes they got so they'd make two trips to Moscow a year, but that was the big one in the fall to get the winter supply. They'd get everything fixed up. They had their food canned from the garden, and their potatoes dug in the cellar and all this. They were just about holed up in the winter the way he used to tell it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I've often wondered about how much turnover there was among these real earliest pioneers, because they say, what they say and what happened in a lot of places, what you read about is that there was a lot of turnover and that maybe one in ten actually wound up staying. And yet most people seem to, you know, those people that stayed seem to remember it as a lot more stable than I just wondered.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, I think, from looking back, I think probably the population was pretty stable there for several years, because there's two of us old families that's still out there now. I mean, the Morays, Mrs. Cecil Shamer was one of the Morays, their place joined ours, and their father came, and the homestead a year or two or three before my father homesteaded.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: But I think there was quite a few of them stayed. Maybe when you look back there wasn't too big a percent, but I imagine 50, 60 percent of them stayed several years away. It's just from listening to my father and my grandfather talking. I was thinking maybe some of these real early people, like say the guy that was squatting on his place, you know, there might have been a lot of people like that that were here right at the very beginning, and they were the ones that turned over. Yeah, I imagine, of course, I probably made a lot of turnover. Now, I think he just came there and he put up a shack and he was going to go into a homestead and prove up on it, and he just decided it was too hard. So then he pulls out and my grandfather picks it up, and I think there were several places like that. Maybe that was, caused a lot of the moving people. They decided it was going to be too hard to deal with. And we would leave.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in the winters, and when you were a kid, did things get really slow? I mean, was there a lot of time that your parents couldn't be that busy, or were they really busy in the winter too?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, they almost had livestock and they had to be taken care of. So dad used to raise hogs, and he used to have quite a few of them. And they had, after they, later when I was a kid, they had these potlatch forest lumber camps, one just south of Deary, and one, they weren't too far off. And he used to butcher hogs in the wintertime and deliver butchered hogs to these lumber camps. So he kept pretty busy. Of course, it might not have been that way with some of the families, but he used to, I remember he used to do lots of butchering in the wintertime. He used to have, oh, butchered, two weeks probably. When they butchered in those days, whenever you butchered, the neighbors all come in and help you butcher, and you'd go help them butcher. But he, I remember, he used to butcher several hogs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the pace was about the same as the rest of the year, or would you say it would be more leisure, more leisure than the pace?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh, I think they lived a lot, didn't move near as fast as we do, but they kept busy. They made wood, some of them made wood in the winter, and some of them made posts. They had their winter jobs they did in the winter, but I don't think they hit it quite as hard as we do now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he do a lot of repair and work in the winter then?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Some of them, they didn't have shops on farms then like they did now, and it wasn't too much to fix up a game flower, a harrow draw bar, and stuff like that. It didn't take very long to do the repair on machinery. Of course, the binders, when they got those, they were repairing, but the binders were small compared to a combine, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any much concern in your family? Was he very concerned, do you think, about the prospects of being able to make good on the place?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, I'm wondering how close to the bone it was. Like I had heard after the First World War, there was a pretty bad falling off in the prices in the early 20s.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, it was pretty rough. And there during the Depression, when we were three of us kids in college, he'd gotten up and he'd been on this board, bank board of directors, and he got to be president of the bank, and the bank closed, and I think he only had $75 to his name. And it was pretty tough going, I tell you. I quit school, I cut school that year, didn't, last semester, went home, and we lined up all the beef cows, we couldn't milk cows, and that time we had a cream station in Kendrick, and sold cream and so forth. And there was pretty rough times then, but he always knew it was going to be better someday. I know the difference, I always remember my uncle. He always said, oh, the country's going to the devil. Oh, let's see, I'm going to make it. And he said, oh, here, look here, Amy. It's not that bad so far. Dad always figured he was going to get better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he call him, Amy?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: His name was Amos, and that was his brother -in -law, and he'd call him Amy. Probably never could convince him, though. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was it, what did happen in the bank? What kind of situation was it that developed that he lost?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, what happened was when this depression came, and people got scared and they started drawing their money out, and the banks had loans on it, and had the money loaned out, and they couldn't liquidate fast enough to pay off depositors. That's what closed the banks in the depression area. If people would, if they would have just left their money in the bank and stayed with them, they wouldn't have any trouble. But you see what happens. You got a loan out here doing six months, and the guy comes in and wants to get money. And you can't liquidate, and that guy can't dig up the money right now, so that's what happens to the bank. People just pull in their savings out of the bank's rate. The banks are going to go both. It's sort of a, like the depression, there's sort of got to be a chain thing. Well, one bank goes broke, and everybody rushes into the next bank, and other banks start drawing their money in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this the, which Kendrick bank was this?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Kendrick State Bank. Kendrick State. We did it closed down before the bank holiday, or as it before Roosevelt County.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, but they opened it again. Later on, then the two banks went together down and made one bank, but it closed about a month before that bank holiday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the losses of the, at the point that it closed?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, actually, it came out pretty good, actually, because it had to be liquidated. But they liquidated out nearly 100%.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yes. It wasn't, they had good paper, good mortgage, and good notes, and stuff like that. It was just the thing that you just can't liquidate them fast enough when the people draw their money in. So it took over a period of years, but they pretty well liquidated out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that mean that the people that owed money paid back?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Right. And the way they got it started again, they got some of the large stockholders, and they had to say, they called it big money then, $78 ,000, to go in and put their money as stock, and they got the thing to go in again that way, and these were stockholders, and they came out all right. The people that did that came out real good, because later on the bank was sold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many years later did they start it up again?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, it started up the next year after they sold. It wasn't closed very long. I don't think it was closed six months.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how long did it take your father to get his money back on what he had in it?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, I think within a year or so they had their money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What he had in it?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, he lost some, I believe, but not too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like it wasn't too bad then.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I heard the Moscow State Bank only paid back some 50 cents on the dollar. Well, there were several of them that didn't, but I think that one particular bank, I think most everybody got their money on it in the long run. Pretty sure they did.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: It seems to me that that's also what happened in Troy, although the bank didn't close. Nobody told me. That bank was just about, way with Troy, they just told their depositors that they threw out the money and wouldn't have a bank in them. The depositors stuck with him. The only man that was in there for years, you know, they were just about ready to close. But they kept it open with them, talking to people a lot of them, taking their money out. And Frank Green down there helped me out a lot. He used it pretty well all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you think that maybe if, say, they'd have tried a little harder, and you can't hurt me if they'd have moved down it faster, they could have, these things, when they get going, get ready for it. Well, it's hard to combat something like that, see.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: It's hard to see. One thing that sort of hurt them there at the Kennedy State Bank, the Bank of Juliaetta was owned by the, well, it's the first security now, was Melgarden had that old bank. And they took, they bought that Juliaetta bank. And it wasn't going very good, you see. And those people were the first to pull their money out. They didn't, some of them didn't like it, but they're losing their bank, and they didn't realize what kind of predicament they really in, see. Some of those people were the first to pull their money out, so that hurt them. Out of the Kennedy Bank. Yeah. They started into the Juliaetta bank. After the Juliaetta bank was closed, see. The Kennedy Bank bought the Juliaetta bank, and so that moved all the depositors, all their paper, and so forth in. Oh, I see. So when this depression started going, and some of them didn't like them losing their bank in Juliaetta, so they were probably the first that started drawing their money out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it Kennedy State that bought the Juliaetta bank?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yes. And that was, and that had been Melgarden's bank. Yeah, Melgarden's bank, yeah. Well, and there were two banks in Kennedy, too. Right, yeah. The Farmers Bank and the Kennedy State Bank. My dad was a stockholder at the Farmers Bank, too, but I mean, not very much. Just one share. There's $100 shares in those days, I think it was. Well, that might have also put more strain on the community resources to have two banks to support instead of one. Right. Mike Troy got away with this one. Yeah, right, yeah. See, at one time Troy had two banks, too, but they just had one bank at this time. And that made a difference, too, yeah. I'll say it did. And the other bank was a branch of the, it was a Balmer bank, a branch of the Balmer system in Lewis. And of course, they had their backing down there of the bigger bank, and they kept it from closing its doors, but it wasn't very good shape either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there, I was just mentioning Balmer, people that, and you know, in the early days, did Balmer have a kind of bad reputation around Kendrick?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh, yes, yes, they did. There was some Balmer land. Well, I joined the places I have now in the Balmer place over on, yeah, they joined on the east, west side of the ridge, too. I think probably the reason, I don't know how this happened, but undoubtedly they probably loaned people money and got, and then they couldn't pay their bill and they'd foreclose on their land. And that's, they got a pretty big holding, and I probably, probably some of the people just walked off and left it, too. And probably some of them had foreclosed. I know they foreclosed on one guy, lived over there by us. And they had an old guy, he called him S .D. White, used to be the land manager for Balmer when I was a kid. And he, oh, he was hard -boiled. I imagine he was pretty rough on some of these renters they had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he actually come out of people's places? Is that how you saw him?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, I used to see him quite often when I was a kid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Collect, you think?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, I mean, when they didn't pay their rent and that kind of stuff. I remember seeing him a lot. I remember one time that he called my dad up over the phone. We had the thrashers there, and they had this big machine. Right, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I remember my dad was saying, you will?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: He says, well, we're ready to go. That's all dad said. I asked him after what he said. He said, S .D. White said that he was going to put the whole board of directors of Rook Valley warehousing in jail because they had bought some crop off of one of these places. And the thing that dad has said is...

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: It wasn't blocked. Right, yeah. Right after blocked. Yeah. But I know he used to stop and talk to dad once in a while, and they really ribbed each arm. I remember that just kidding about somehow. A lot of hard -boiled guy he was. I think that's where they really got the reputation, probably this one old guy he was, hard as mail. I suppose in those days maybe they had to be, I don't know. They wouldn't stay in business very long nowadays, I don't think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, when you were growing up, do you have any memories of prohibition as growing up, as the moonshiners?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh, yeah. We had moonshine, we had moonshine. There was moonshine around a lot when I was a kid.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I remember talking about it a lot.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I used to catch a still once in a while out in that country, coming down the woods someplace.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father's being opposed to any drink make you feel that you couldn't try it?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, I don't know. I never had any desire to try it.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: While the kids did, I'd seen lots of it, but I just never wanted to try it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when your father's teaching?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Must have been, must have been he's teaching. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much opportunity was there for the, do you think, for kids that were growing up there, to stay around Kendrick and the Ridge? You know, was they with these big families in the small farms growing up?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it really wasn't too much.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: The way it seemed to work out, the kids that went on to school, went on to college, never came back with the ones that never went to school, after high school, or some of them actually stayed around, stayed home, I mean, stayed in the community until they'd find something else, maybe get into some other field. But there just, there wasn't too much work for a big employment in the area. Of course, they always had, a lot of them went to the timber work too, so they'd leave the home to go work in the woods, see the potlatch forest, and some of them had these camps, and down the area, and so a lot of them went, someone went there, but staying right in their own community, there wasn't much for them to do. That's a farm. I know there was one family there that all their kids stayed on the farm, and they'd watch, and maybe they'd get to rent a farm or something, get married, and live there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, at your time, most of the kids go on to high school from the eighth grade, when you were of age?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not, none of the biggest, not, I was a small percent really.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: In college, boy, that was out of this world. I think, oh, my high school class, which wasn't very big, seven people graduated high school, same year, so I did. I think I was the only one who went on to college. I was one or two I went, let's say, when I went to business college, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you settling going to college? Was it your idea or your father?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, I just grew up with the idea that I wanted to go to college. My mother had been a school teacher. I guess it was just environment I grew up. I just asked her to go, that was the thing I wanted to do.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: And all of our, there were four of us, and we all went to college. I was the only one that never graduated. I got my four years in. I went, I quit the mid -year in 32, went into depression, went home. And then I came back in the fall of 33, and so I got my number of years. I never got the right semesters in. So, but my brother went on and graduated from ag school, and then he came back and he and I farmed together, and then he went back and got his master's degree in soil. And my sister, one of them was a commercial teacher. She taught school several years, and my sister was all mag graduated. She taught two or three years before she got married. And then they went to New Mexico and her husband worked for Indian service, and so she taught at home ec.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then, what was it, Albuquerque or one of them?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: When I was in Albuquerque, it was Gallop. Gallop. Navajo. Yeah, Navajo. She taught at home ec there several years. So, I don't know. We were, it was some of, we sort of had some resentment toward us kids in the local community about us going on to school with certain people, but we never let it bother too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was Idaho like to you when you went to school here?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, it was a lot smaller, and I think we had between 1500, 1600 pupils, maybe. And in those days, you know, everybody said hello to everybody. And it was his little handbook that had hello, most friendly bunch of people I ever saw.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Quite a change from high school coming, but it didn't take us long to blend in. High State is what the old Lindley Hall is torn down now. It was a dormitory then. Later on, they made a coop. I thought I wasn't coop when I was going to the state there. Second year after I was here, I got to working in the kitchen and hashing and scrubbing the floor and stuff like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, you were working your way through school at that time?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I was almost self -supporting then, after that, because, well, see, my brother was just one year behind me and my sister one year behind him. And then it ended up, when I was a junior, about three of us up here, my brother got a job working for Deary, and my sister got a job working for the veterinary at that time for the university working for the room and boarding the home. So, it wasn't a real big outlay, but it was an effort. We always had the support of our parents about going on to school, because they never forced us. We just sort of grew up with that attitude that we wanted an education.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't get into a fraternity, though?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: No, I never joined a fraternity. My brother joined the Teaks later on, and my sister, neither sister joined the sorority either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That cost more money, did it?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, it cost more money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a real, was that a lot of why you didn't, were you interested or weren't you interested in it anyway?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, I wasn't too much interested, really. Well, I think one thing, the costs are above, and I just didn't have a desire for a fraternity. Like some kids that come here, and if they didn't get to go to a fraternity, they thought there was something bad about them. I always remember one of our proctors, Bob Green, he's still living here around someplace. I don't know, George Green. He got up in our dormitory in the dining room, and he says, it's Rice Week now. Some of you people aren't going to get a chance to go to a fraternity, and some aren't. Some are going to go to a fraternity, and he's going to go to their head, and they won't know you when you go to the fraternity. He says, remember, if he's that kind of a person, he's not worth a tinker's damn.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I thought about that a lot, and I knew some of the guys at Pledge were just as friendly. Good friends actually went to the maturing, and others, it did go to their head. They thought that was something really out of this room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel that you were here just to learn, or did you feel you were here to have a good time?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I felt I was coming here to learn. That was the idea of coming. We had some good times, you were bound to have some good times, but basically, we were supposed to learn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the kinds of social good times that they had then?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, we used to have, I don't know whether they still do or not, have visitations. Dinner hours, and by the dinner of gals over to dinner at our dormitory, and they'd dance afterwards, and then you'd be invited over to their place maybe the next month or something. Then we had their hall dances, and there was also the dance down at the student union, and the class dances. I learned to dance after I got up here too. I don't know, it seemed like we went to lots of shows. Then I was quite active in the church group, that was the Wesley Foundation, the Memphis church group. In fact, that's where I met my wife. We had a party every Saturday night, and social hours Sunday night, and we had Sunday school in the morning, and so forth. It seemed like there was plenty of places to go to, because we didn't have a good time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she going to school here too?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yes, she was in the same time I was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From where?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: She was from Boise Valley down at Eagle, close to Boise. Her name was Warren, and the Warren family had the most number of students from one family in the university. The family name of Johnson came along and had one or two more. Her brother,

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: her older brother was the first, next to her older brother was the first president of Lindley Hall, and the first building, Charlie Warren. Then she had another older brother named George, who graduated from law school. He's still living, he's up in his 80s in California.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a good deal of supervision of you, or young men and women when you were at the university then, or did you pretty much either go your own way?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, they did. They controlled the girls. The girls had to be in certain hours, and that sort of controlled the boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess they had to be in my midnight, didn't they?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I don't know, but Dean French had quite a reputation still. Oh, she was quite a young girl. I remember when I was pregnant, she came over to our hall and had this big deal on etiquette that she used to give every year. Sometimes twice a year she'd come. We had to proctor saw that we was in again. Here Dean French's lessons on etiquette, how to behave yourselves and all this kind of stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get much out of it at the time, do you think?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh, I guess we did. Of course, my mother was pretty strict on manners, table and stuff like that. That wasn't too shocking to me as it was to some, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it helped some?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Oh, yeah, it helped. We had a lot of fun about it, but it helped. I know it did. It had to help. I've heard people say that it was common for the boys to hate Dean French the whole time they were in school, and then after they got out, turned around and just think she was the greatest person there. Yeah, they give her hell all the time, but she was pretty good at reading. She was really so hard. If you'd go in her office and ask her about something, she's real nice to you then. But, boy, at these meetings, she really laid it down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that there was much fooling around and sitting together before marriage like there is these days?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Not too much. We had a little bit of it. Not too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not common like you are. Not common, though.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: No idea. It was all about common. That's one thing that really seems to change. Yeah, it seems like it sure changed a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the Depression much of a pall over the way people were at that time? Did it seem to affect the students at the school?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because it was already starting, I know.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, I expected it. I remember one kid that came to the school, he played basketball because he got a scholarship, came here in an old Ford car, and he didn't have enough money to run it. We'd throw our pennies together. Sometimes we'd buy a gallon of gas, and we'd toot in that old car, which one time I remember we had to push it downtown. It used to be a service station down at the foot of the hill down here where the electric company is down there. Well, price drawers down there. There was one down in there. We'd push it down the hill and we'd pull our money and get our gas. It was about 23 cents or 18 or 19 cents. We'd get our pennies together and maybe buy two gallons of gas. We had a lot of fun. Everybody was the same, though. Everybody was hard up. I don't know how we had just much fun then. It didn't have money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you studying then?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I was taking business.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Took medicine and ended up farming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you weren't planning then at the time really to farm?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: No, I was going to go in the bank, but I was really intended to do.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: After I went home, Dad wanted me to stay and help him farm. I was going to help farm until my brother, which is an egg,

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: came over to take over the farm. He came there a year or two and then he decided he wanted to go back to school to get his master's degree. I don't know. I just naturally drifted into it. When the war came, early 40s, Dad said, I'll just turn the farm over to you if you want to farm. That's how I got to farm. I never intended to farm, but I have regrets at all. It's quite a business. Business education sure didn't hurt me in the farming field. I always worked real closely with the county extension agent and the ASC program and so forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So your brother didn't want to go back to farming then?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: No, not really. When he graduated in soils, he went to work for the soil conservation service. Then he got drafted in the Army. Then when he came back, he went to work for Libby's. He was a fieldman and a fire down in the coast for over 20 years for them. They retired him. He'd have a real estate and he's working for a Northwest packing company down in Woodward now on the lean. He quit it a while. He just seemed to like to get back in. He's quite a social guy and he knows his stuff in that line. We're vying for packers or fruit and vegetables and so forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have an in mind then to stay here if you possibly could?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, I intended to go in the banking home when my dad was on the board. Really.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I had a chance to go to work there once and my uncle sort of, well, the guy was going to have to be out of town. The banker was going to have to be out of town. There was only one man then. The one man was gone. They had to have somebody there to be in the bank by himself. There was another fellow that worked in the bank for years that was unemployed. So they hung on. This guy had the experience they better get him in the bank and then he got the second job after this guy came back. He had to be gone a while. I just about got started there then. Then I got married, 35 I was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Things were pretty severe for you, I imagine, for your folks to decide that you really should come home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or did you decide you should leave school?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, yeah, I sort of decided. My grades weren't too good and I worried quite a lot about things, you know. My grades weren't very good that semester. Dad just had to have some help.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were worried about the farm?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, worried about, you know. So I just stayed home. I went home that mid -year. It was a pretty tough pick and I tell you, the bank had closed that in December I guess that year.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Then I went back in fall of 33 again. My wife graduated in the spring of 32. My brother graduated in 33. He came back the farm about three or four years I guess. He used to get so excited when things were pushing, you know. He wouldn't sleep at night. He'd got to get this done and get that done. So he decided he wanted to go into something else. I think he had a mind getting going with solid conservation because he got his masters in soil. Well, when you say things were pretty tough, I'm slim pickings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that mean that there wasn't much that you could do?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pretty much money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do then? Just work the farm with the farm?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Worked the farm, yeah. I did a little bit of everything where I could find something to do. I even hung some paper in rooms. People worked on telephone lines. All kinds of things that had to be done. Just what I could get. Two dollars a day, you know. Don't get money very fast.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you wind up taking over a lot of the farming pretty soon after you got out of school then?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yes. Dad shifted it. So I died after I came back. He put a lot of responsibility on me around the farm actually, really. I'll say about the time I got married, it was very four and five in there. Of course my brother was there in 34, but I sort of took the lead on things. He just shifted quite a lot of responsibility on the farm over to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When would you say it started really recovering when you started really coming out of the Depression?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I suppose.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: 37 or 89 and through there it started picking up. It was 25 and 35 cents a bushel. I was right in the deep depression. Don't bring money in very fast.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you selling it? 25 a bushel?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Well, we never did sell it in 25. Some of the neighbors did. Some of us sold it 35 cents. I don't think we ever sold anything a little 50, but I don't remember what the exact figure was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know any of your neighbors who were real dire straits?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I mean, really, really barely had enough to eat. Oh, yeah. Generally, the farm people generally had enough to eat. They had their garden, their food, and their candelas. They had their own meat. They had animals they could kill. They never went hungry. They just didn't have any money to do anything else. They stayed at home in 8.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did anybody lose their places that you knew?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, there were two or three.

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: There were a few lost places. I know the neighbor, he was going to throw his place up. He had a federal land bank in the know. He couldn't raise enough money to pay the interest. He had a real good guy here that worked for the federal land bank then. He came out there and he just stayed with him half a day and talked him to the notion of hanging on to his place. He made it all right. He just worried because he couldn't raise it. Most of these guys were honest and they owed something. They wanted to pay it when it was due. Some of them just couldn't take the pressure. There was generally a way to work it out with some banks and some of the other companies were pretty reasonable about it. If you tried. Some of them just figured there was never a chance. So they just quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the people that did give up, maybe didn't have to?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Some of them, if they just stuck it out, they could just stay there. There might have been a few that couldn't have done that. There wasn't too many in our area, but there were some.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about politics in those days?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: I understood that the county and Moscow especially was an extremely strong Republican in those days. Yeah, it was. The county used to be, nearly all of them, the county often were Republican. It seemed like then it switched around and has its change. Democrats had a good election once in a while. I think probably now it's about 50 -50 away it looks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your father a Democrat?

speaker: Gerald Ingle
words: Yeah, he was a Democrat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that really from his father's southern background?
